rationale: Berger and Milkman (2012) is used as a key prior benchmark in the citing paper's theory section on why content gets shared, especially the role of emotional arousal. The citing paper then builds hypotheses and tests around whether that effect holds in live debate tweeting versus postdebate tweeting. In results and discussion, the authors explicitly interpret their findings relative to this benchmark, arguing that emotionality is less important during live events but more important afterward. This is a clear framing and interpretation role, not a passing mention.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Berger and Milkman (2012) as a baseline virality account and then qualifies it by showing a timing-dependent boundary condition in live political events. It finds weaker emotion effects during real-time debate narration and stronger effects after debates, refining when the original emotion-virality pattern applies.
